---
title: "Events, breakpoints, and the ring oscillator"
subtitle: "T4 · Phase T4.3 · `va-transient::events`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: code complete — ladder rung 6, implementation reach closed through the real pipeline.**
QSPICE golden generation was attempted and diagnosed, not silently skipped — see "Limitations."
:::

## Goal

Force the adaptive integrator to land exactly on specific times, and watch for a monitored
quantity crossing a threshold — the machinery a real oscillator demo needs to prove it actually
oscillates, not just "ran without error."

## Where it fits

```
 EventQueue{breakpoints, watches} ──► run_with_events ──► Waveform{t, x, crossings}
```

`run` is a thin wrapper over `run_with_events` with an empty queue — every circuit that doesn't
need forced timepoints or crossing detection pays nothing for this machinery.

## The idea

**A breakpoint clamps the adaptive step, it doesn't reset the controller.** `EventQueue::
next_after(t)` returns the next scheduled breakpoint after the current time; each step attempt
clamps its own candidate landing time to that breakpoint (`t_next.min(bp)`) so the integrator is
guaranteed to solve *exactly* at a source discontinuity or forced timepoint, without discarding
whatever step-size history the LTE controller had already built up.

**Crossing detection is an honest simplification, not full `cross()` semantics** — stated
plainly in `CrossingWatch`'s own doc comment: rather than re-solving at the exact crossing
instant, it checks `x[unknown] - threshold` for a sign change between two consecutive *accepted*
points and linearly interpolates. This is accurate enough precisely because the same LTE control
that bounds the state's error between two accepted points also bounds the interpolation error —
not a coincidence, a consequence of both existing in the same solve loop.

**The ring oscillator is the first circuit here with a genuinely gain-capable device**, and its
DC operating point is a real, *unstable* equilibrium — Newton finds it exactly like any other
fixed point (it has no notion of stability), and the transient's whole point is to diverge away
from it. Two design choices made this tractable rather than merely lucky:

- **Mismatched per-stage component values, not identical ones.** Three identical ring stages
  have an *exactly* symmetric DC equilibrium; a deterministic solver started from a matching
  guess has no noise to break that symmetry the way a real circuit's component tolerances
  always do. Mismatching `Rb`/`Rc` stage to stage means the symmetric point is never an exact
  equilibrium of *this* circuit to begin with.
- **A real surprise, found netlist-driving this circuit for the first time (2026-07-18), not
  assumed from the topology**: the original hand-built fixture nudges the DC solution by 5 mV
  before starting its transient, specifically to break the exact symmetry a deterministic
  solver's own converged answer would otherwise sit at motionlessly. Driving the *same* circuit
  through `va-cli`'s ordinary `x=0` cold start (no such nudge) turns out to do the identical job
  by a different route: every stage sees an *identical* `Vbe` from `x=0`, so all three charge to
  nearly the same forward-active bias within ~12.5 µs — close enough to the symmetric-but-unstable
  equilibrium that the stages' own mismatched `R` values are enough to kick off the same genuine,
  sustained, growing oscillation. Confirmed by inspecting a full run's node trajectories (each
  collector swings between ~0.06 V and ~5 V, repeatedly, staggered per stage) before trusting it —
  a first attempt at checking this by watching only one collector actually *failed* (2 crossings
  in the observed window, not the expected several), simply because that one stage had a quiet
  stretch in its own cycle while the other two (6 and 3 crossings) kept oscillating; the real fix
  was checking the max across all three collectors, not loosening the assertion.

## The code

```rust
pub enum Event { Breakpoint(f64), Crossing(f64) }

pub struct CrossingWatch { pub unknown: usize, pub threshold: f64 }

pub struct EventQueue { /* breakpoints: Vec<f64>, watches: Vec<CrossingWatch> */ }
impl EventQueue {
    pub fn push_breakpoint(&mut self, t: f64);
    pub fn next_after(&self, t: f64) -> Option<f64>;
    pub fn push_watch(&mut self, unknown: usize, threshold: f64);
    pub fn watches(&self) -> &[CrossingWatch];
}
```

The ring topology itself, now real Verilog-A netlist syntax (`circuits/ring_osc.net`,
`t6-integration/01-netlist.qmd`'s new `Q<name> c b e model` element): three RC-coupled
common-emitter stages, each `Q` a `va-abi::reference::Bjt` reached via `va-cli`'s `"bjt"`
`reference_instance` branch (fixed `Is=1e-15`, `βF=100`, `βR=1`, matching the original hand-built
fixture's own values).

## It works

The original, hand-built-instance demo:

```console
$ cargo test -p va-transient ring_oscillator_sustains_oscillation
test integrator::tests::ring_oscillator_sustains_oscillation ... ok
```

checks a deliberately-nudged DC bias grows into real, sustained oscillation (at least two full
crossings of the collector's own DC bias, with the trough deepening over time — genuine growth,
not just alternation). The *same* topology, now driven through the real netlist pipeline from an
ordinary `x=0` cold start:

```console
$ cargo test -p va-cli ring_osc_sustains_oscillation_through_the_real_pipeline
test tests::ring_osc_sustains_oscillation_through_the_real_pipeline ... ok
```

checks the max rail-midpoint crossing count across all three collectors (≥4) and that every
collector's own swing covers most of the 0–5 V rail at some point in the run — not just one
node, since a ring oscillator's per-stage phase shift means any single collector can sit on a
quiet stretch of its own cycle while the ring as a whole keeps oscillating.

## Limitations

**Stated in `integrator.rs`'s own doc comment, found empirically while tuning this circuit's
component values:** as the oscillation grows, it eventually pushes a junction into strong
forward bias on both sides at once, where this simplified Ebers-Moll model's exponential terms
grow large enough that the embedded-pair LTE estimator stops agreeing at any step size. `tstop`
is chosen comfortably inside the confirmed well-behaved region, not at that numerical edge.

**QSPICE golden generation was attempted and diagnosed, not silently skipped.** Translating this
circuit the same way rungs 3–5 were (a native `.model bjt NPN(...)` card, `UIC` + `IC=0` on the
coupling capacitors) runs cleanly but does not reproduce the oscillation: every node in the
QSPICE run sits at essentially exactly `VCC` for the whole window, a non-conducting degenerate
state. This circuit's base/collector voltages are purely *nonlinearly* determined (no linear DC
path, only the BJT's own turn-on characteristic); QSPICE's `UIC` mode only seeds *reactive*
elements, and doesn't re-solve non-reactive nodes the way this project's own `x=0`-seeded Newton
cold start does. Closing this — explicit `.ic V(node)=...` seeding, or some other mechanism — is
real, scoped future work, detailed in `t6-integration/03-validation.qmd`.

## What's next

`t6-integration/01-netlist.qmd` — the netlist parser that turns a `.net` deck (including this
tutorial's `ring_osc.net`) into device instances in the first place.
